result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

There is a town in the south of Portugal where the streets have no names. Not because the residents never bothered to name them, but because the names kept changing—first with the monarchy, then the republic, then the dictatorship, then the revolution—until the people collectively decided that names were unreliable things to give a street, and they stopped using them altogether. Directions are given by landmark: past the blue house, left at the fountain that hasn't worked since 1987, straight until you smell bread.

I've never been to this town. I'm not even certain it exists in the way I've described it. But the idea of it has lived in me since I first encountered something like it in a passage I can no longer source—a fragment that detached from its origin and floated freely into that vast interior space where thoughts marinate and transform into something the original author would never recognize. This is how most of our knowledge actually works, if we're honest. We are less libraries than compost heaps: everything decomposing, recombining, becoming soil for something new.

I want to talk about forgetting.

---

We treat memory as the heroic faculty and forgetting as its failure state. But there is a story about a man—Jorge Luis Borges wrote it—named Funes, who could forget nothing. Every leaf he had ever seen remained in perfect detail. Every moment was preserved in the amber of total recall. And it destroyed him. He couldn't think, because thinking requires abstraction, and abstraction requires the merciful blurring of particulars. To see that this dog and that dog are both *dogs*, you must forget, at least momentarily, the specific curl of this one's tail, the particular shade of that one's ear. Thought is, in some fundamental sense, the structured forgetting of detail in favor of pattern.

I find this idea vertiginous.

It suggests that intelligence is not accumulation but curation—that the mind's deepest work happens not when it's acquiring but when it's releasing. Every night, while you sleep, your brain is pruning synapses, dissolving the connections that didn't earn their keep during the day. You wake up lighter. You wake up, in a very real sense, as less than you were—and this is what makes you functional. The sculptor removes marble. The editor removes words. The mind removes memories. Creation through subtraction.

But we resist this. God, how we resist this.

The entire architecture of the modern internet is a monument to the terror of forgetting. We screenshot conversations. We archive tweets. We save articles to reading lists we'll never revisit—digital towers of intention, mausoleums of "I'll get to it later." Every photograph is a small act of defiance against time, a fist raised at entropy, a whispered *no* to the current that carries everything downstream. And yet the photographs pile up in our phones by the thousands, unreviewed, unsorted, a comprehensive record of everything and a meaningful account of nothing.

There's a paradox here that I think about often: the more we record, the less we remember. Not despite the recording but *because* of it. When you know the photograph exists, you give yourself permission to let the moment pass through you unmetabolized. The camera becomes a surrogate for attention. You were there, technically. The image proves it. But the experience itself slipped through you like water through a net, because the net was pointed at a screen instead of stretched across the moment.

I'm not making the tired argument that phones are bad and we should all be more present. That sermon has been preached to death, and mostly by people who say it while maintaining an aggressive Instagram presence. What I'm interested in is something more structural: the way that external memory systems change the *kind* of animal we are.

---

Before writing, knowledge lived in bodies. It lived in the rhythms of epic poetry, in the cadences that made Homer portable—wine-dark sea, rosy-fingered dawn, swift-footed Achilles—those repeated epithets weren't decorative. They were mnemonic. They were handles. The entire technology of oral poetry was a technology of memory: how to store vast quantities of narrative in the only available medium, which was the meat of the human brain.

Then writing arrived, and Socrates—if we believe Plato, which is already a hall of mirrors—complained that it would make people forgetful. They would rely on external marks rather than internal cultivation. They would have the appearance of wisdom without its substance. They would become, in a word, shallow.

He was right, of course. And he was also completely wrong. Writing did make us forgetful in the way he feared. But it freed up cognitive space for entirely new kinds of thinking—the long chains of reasoning that are nearly impossible to maintain in working memory alone, the complex arguments that need to be laid out on a surface and examined, revised, restructured. Writing didn't replace thinking. It *changed* what thinking could be.

Every new memory technology does this. The printing press. The library. The encyclopedia. The search engine. Each one outsources some function that was previously internal, and each time, something is lost and something is gained, and the people living through the transition mourn the loss and are blind to the gain, or celebrate the gain and are blind to the loss, because that is how people are.

We are living through another such transition now, and I don't think anyone fully understands its shape yet.

---

Let me turn to a different kind of forgetting: the kind that cultures do.

Every nation has an mythology, and every mythology is a carefully constructed pattern of remembering and forgetting. The United States remembers the Revolution and forgets the genocide. France remembers the Resistance and forgets the collaboration. Britain remembers the war and forgets the empire—or rather, remembers the empire selectively, bathed in a golden light that obscures the blood. These are not accidental omissions. They are architectural. They are load-bearing. Remove them, and the structure of national identity begins to groan and shift in ways that terrify the people living inside it.

This is why fights about history curricula are so vicious. They are never really about history. They are about the present-tense question of who we are, which depends entirely on the story we tell about where we came from. Change the story, and you change the identity, and identity is the thing people will kill and die for more readily than anything else.

But here's what interests me: cultural forgetting is not always malicious. Sometimes it is necessary in the way that personal forgetting is necessary—as a precondition for functioning. After a civil war, after an atrocity, after a period of mutual horror, the survivors face an impossible choice: remember everything and be paralyzed by it, or forget enough to go on living. South Africa tried to split the difference with the Truth and Reconciliation Commission—a structured remembering designed to enable a structured forgetting. Tell the truth, and the truth will set you free. Not free from the past, but free from the past's grip on the future.

Did it work? The answer depends on who you ask and what you mean by "work." The country didn't descend into the race war that everyone predicted. But the inequality persists, the wounds suppurate, the forgetting was perhaps too efficient for some and not efficient enough for others. There is no clean solution to the problem of historical trauma. There is only the ongoing negotiation between memory and mercy, between justice and peace, between the debt owed to the dead and the life owed to the living.

---

I think about this at the personal level too. I think about the relationships that end, and the slow, strange process by which a person who was once the center of your world becomes first a memory, then an abstraction, then a kind of character in the story you tell about your own life—a character whose lines you've probably rewritten, whose motivations you've simplified, who has become more symbol than person. This is not dishonesty. It is the inevitable consequence of the fact that you cannot carry another person's full complexity inside you forever. You carry a sketch. A compression. A lossy file.

And sometimes you encounter the actual person again—at a grocery store, at a mutual friend's wedding—and the distance between your memory of them and the reality of them is so vast that it induces a kind of vertigo. They got older, obviously. But it's not that. It's that they're *dimensional* again. They have that habit you forgot about, that way of laughing, that thing they do with their hands when they're nervous. For a moment, the compressed file expands, and you feel the full weight of a person who has been living a complete life in your absence, accumulating experiences you know nothing about, becoming someone your memory never predicted.

And then you go home, and the file compresses again, and they return to their place in the narrative, and you go on.

This is forgetting as kindness. If we carried every person we'd ever loved at full resolution, we would collapse under the weight of it. The heart has a carrying capacity, and it is not infinite.

---

I want to say something about landscapes.

I grew up near the ocean—or rather, let me not claim autobiography I don't have. Let me instead imagine someone who grew up near the ocean, because the point I want to make is not about any specific ocean but about the relationship between place and memory.

This imagined person returns, after twenty years, to the coast where they spent their childhood. The sea is the same sea. The rocks are, give or take some erosion, the same rocks. But the person standing on them is not the same person who scrambled over them at age eight, and so the landscape is not the same landscape, because a landscape is not just a physical arrangement of matter—it is a relationship between a place and a consciousness, and when the consciousness changes, the landscape changes with it.

What this person is experiencing is not the place. It is the *gap* between their memory of the place and the place itself. And that gap is, in a strange way, the most honest measure of how much they have changed. The rocks don't care. The sea doesn't remember. Only the human stands there, caught between two versions of the same scene, unable to fully inhabit either one.

This is nostalgia: not the love of the past, but the ache of the gap. The word comes from the Greek *nostos* (homecoming) and *algos* (pain). The pain of homecoming. The pain of discovering that you can go back to a place but you cannot go back to a time, because time is the one river that absolutely will not let you step in it twice.

---

I've been circling something, and I want to try to land on it now.

I think the reason forgetting frightens us so much is that we suspect, at some deep level, that we *are* our memories. That the self is nothing more than a pattern of recollections, a story we tell from a particular vantage point, and that if the memories go, the self goes with them. This is the horror of dementia—not the loss of information, but the loss of *identity*. The person is still there, breathing, warm, present. But the story has come apart, and without the story, we don't know who they are, and more terrifyingly, they don't know who they are.

But I want to push back against this, gently. Because I've seen people with advanced dementia who have lost the narrative thread entirely—who cannot tell you their name or their history or the names of their children—and who still respond to music. Who still laugh. Who still reach for a hand. Who still, in some way that precedes and exceeds narrative, *are*.

Maybe the self is not a story. Maybe the story is just one expression of the self—the most articulate expression, perhaps, but not the most fundamental. Maybe underneath the narrative, underneath the memories, underneath the entire elaborate architecture of identity, there is something simpler and more durable. A quality of attention. A way of being in the world. A warmth.

I don't know what to call it. The word *soul* carries too much theological baggage for some people, and not enough for others. Let me just call it *the thing that remains when the story falls away*. I've seen it. I believe in it. Not as a matter of faith but as a matter of observation.

---

So perhaps forgetting is not the enemy. Perhaps it is the slow revelation of what was always underneath—the gradual clearing away of accumulated identity, like wind eroding sandstone to reveal the harder rock beneath. Perhaps what we lose, when we lose our memories, is not ourselves but our *idea* of ourselves, which was always, at best, a rough sketch, a working hypothesis, a map that was never the territory.

And perhaps the town in Portugal had it right. Perhaps the names were always unreliable. Perhaps the real way to navigate is by smell and texture and the color of a house, by the things that persist when the labels change, by the landmarks that don't depend on anyone's authority to be what they are.

Past the blue house. Left at the broken fountain. Straight until you smell bread.

You'll know it when you arrive.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
